output.content: The Fusion Report - By Ignition Research The Impact of AI and the Data Center Goldrush on Non-Data Center Construction Costs June 25, 2026 | AI , Data Centers The AI data center buildout is pushing up costs well beyond electricity and water. A recent study finds it is also raising the price of construction materials and skilled labor while absorbing capital that non-data-center projects, including commercial fusion, are competing for. The Fusion Report looks at who pays, who benefits, and what the $6.7 trillion data center forecast means for fusion’s race for investment. Read More E&P Technologies: Putting the Lightning into Capacitors for Fusion and Aerospace June 23, 2026 | Fusion Supply Chain This Week’s Fusion News: June 19, 2026 June 19, 2026 | Fusion Energy China EAST Gets Set for Ignition in 2027 June 18, 2026 | China , Fusion Energy STAY INFORMED The fusion revolution is happening fast. Get the latest fusion energy news, breakthroughs, and analysis delivered straight to your inbox. Success! Email Subscribe China , Fusion Energy China EAST Gets Set for Ignition in 2027 by Michael Heumann / June 18, 2026 China’s Experimental Advanced Superconducting Tokamak (EAST), the record-setting “Artificial Sun” in Hefei, is now scheduled to attempt fusion ignition in 2027, a milestone reached only once before, by Lawrence Livermore’s National Ignition Facility in 2022. If successful, EAST would be the first to achieve ignition in a steady-state magnetic confinement device, though Commonwealth Fusion Systems’ SPARC is racing toward the same goal on the same timeline. The Fusion Report breaks down what ignition actually requires (the Lawson Criteria of temperature, density, and confinement time) and assesses how close each machine really is. EAST leads on pathfinding plasma physics while SPARC targets Q > 1, but neither has yet crossed into a self-heating burning plasma. The bigger question: would a 2027 ignition milestone finally ignite broad investor interest across the fusion sector? Read More Data Centers Putting Data Centers Into Space: Is That Really The Best Idea Elon Musk Has For SpaceX? by Michael Heumann / June 16, 2026 With SpaceX freshly public and its stock up 28% over the IPO price, talk of putting AI data centers in orbit is back in the headlines. The Fusion Report looks past the one obvious upside, nearly unlimited solar power in a sun-synchronous orbit, and tallies the costs that doom the idea: $160M to $280M per megawatt just to launch, two-year hardware refresh cycles, dense low-earth-orbit debris, space weather, and the near-impossibility of radiating away server heat in a vacuum. Against a ship-borne data center that fits in a shipping container and cools itself for a fraction of the price, orbital compute simply doesn’t pencil out. The verdict: outside of analyzing data that originates in space, orbital data centers are not a great idea. Read More Fusion Energy This Week’s Fusion News: June 12, 2026 by Frankie Berry / June 12, 2026

transcript.raw_model_output: {"action": "finish", "answer": "The latest developments in fusion energy include China's \"Artificial Sun\" (EAST) aiming to achieve ignition in 2027, a milestone that would make it the first to achieve ignition in a steady-state magnetic confinement device. Commonwealth Fusion Systems' SPARC is also racing toward the same goal on the same timeline. According to The Fusion Report, EAST leads on pathfinding plasma physics while SPARC targets Q > 1, but neither has yet crossed into a self-heating burning plasma. Additionally, there are discussions about putting data centers in space, but it's not considered a viable idea due to high costs and challenges. The fusion sector is awaiting the outcome of these developments, which could potentially ignite broad investor interest. (https://thefusionreport.com/)"}

answer: The latest developments in fusion energy include China's "Artificial Sun" (EAST) aiming to achieve ignition in 2027, a milestone that would make it the first to achieve ignition in a steady-state magnetic confinement device. Commonwealth Fusion Systems' SPARC is also racing toward the same goal on the same timeline. According to The Fusion Report, EAST leads on pathfinding plasma physics while SPARC targets Q > 1, but neither has yet crossed into a self-heating burning plasma. Additionally, there are discussions about putting data centers in space, but it's not considered a viable idea due to high costs and challenges. The fusion sector is awaiting the outcome of these developments, which could potentially ignite broad investor interest. (https://thefusionreport.com/)
